notable.notable_blurb: surgeon, 26th Massachusetts Infantry, Department of the Gulf
notable.primary_url_label: Obituary, New England Journal of Medicine, January 8, 1874

education.notes: Harvard Medical School M.D. 1855 per EH 1909 #2760.

career.notes: Per EH: 'connected with his father in practice in East Cambridge, and after his father's death succeeded to a large practice.'

military_service.notes: Commissioned surgeon September 10, 1861. Served in Department of the Gulf (Ship Island, Mississippi, then New Orleans area following its capture April 1862). Resigned June 18, 1862 due to disability from exhausting tropical diseases. First of young surgeons to offer services to Governor John Andrew at the start of the war per NEJM obituary January 8, 1874. Returned to state service in charge of military camps, May 1863. Tribute from Surgeon General Dale at his passing.

institutions.institution_blurb: Harvard College A.B. 1851 and Harvard Medical School M.D. 1855.

cross_connections.display_label: Father and East Cambridge physician partner before his death

narrative_blocks.header: Succeeded father's practice; died New Year's Eve 1873
narrative_blocks.body: After Harvard College 1851 and Harvard Medical School 1855, Anson Parker joined his father Dr. Anson Hooker in practice at East Cambridge — the third consecutive Hooker physician in the Westhampton-to-Cambridge line. He died December 31, 1873 at forty-four, New Year's Eve, leaving a large practice he had held only since his father's 1869 death.

narrative_blocks.header: One twin died; one outlived Cotton Mather's descendants
narrative_blocks.body: On March 15, 1866, twin daughters Elizabeth Mather and Mary Marshall were born at East Cambridge; Mary Marshall died four days later. Elizabeth Mather survived and was identified by Edward Hooker in his 1909 genealogy as the last living descendant of Cotton Mather.

narrative_blocks.header: First surgeon to volunteer; disability hastened death
narrative_blocks.body: Anson Parker Hooker was the first of the young surgeons to offer his services to Governor John Andrew at the outbreak of the Civil War, receiving his commission to the 26th Massachusetts on September 10, 1861, and serving in the Department of the Gulf. Exhausting tropical diseases forced his resignation June 18, 1862 — and the resulting disability was cited in his New England Journal of Medicine obituary as having hastened his death at forty-four.

parents.father_research_notes: Hon. Patrick Boies of Westfield MA per EH 1909 #2760.

narrative_blocks.header: Born leap day 1864; died at twenty-two days
narrative_blocks.body: Mark Marshall Hooker was born February 29, 1864 — a leap day — at East Cambridge, the first child of Anson Parker and Rebecca Boies Hooker. He died March 23, 1864 at twenty-two days old. His middle name Marshall honored his maternal grandmother Orpha Marshall Boies.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Marshall honors maternal grandmother Orpha Marshall Boies of Westfield MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Named last living Cotton Mather descendant, 1909
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Mather Hooker was identified in Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy as 'the last living descendant from Cotton Mather' — the Puritan minister whose Magnalia Christi Americana celebrated Thomas Hooker as a founder of New England. The lineage through which this Mather descent runs has not yet been traced in project sources and warrants verification.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Mather signals descent from Cotton Mather (1663-1728), Puritan minister and author; lineage not yet fully traced in project sources.

narrative_blocks.header: Elizabeth Mather's twin; died four days after birth
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Marshall Hooker was born March 15, 1866 at East Cambridge — the same day as her twin sister Elizabeth Mather. She died March 19, 1866 at four days old; her twin Elizabeth survived and was later named the last living descendant of Cotton Mather.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Marshall honors maternal grandmother Orpha Marshall Boies of Westfield MA.

marriages.notes: EH records unnamed infant (b. and d. 1832) by this marriage — non-standard EH ID '2760a'; no JSON entry created pending compiler decision on alphanumeric IDs.

career.notes: Per EH 1909: 'not only became noted as a physician but was also prominent in the public affairs of the place.'
Representative of East Cambridge in the Massachusetts General Court per EH 1909.

cross_connections.display_label: Studied medicine at East Cambridge; died of hospital disease, 1858
Son who joined his practice; died New Year's Eve 1873

narrative_blocks.header: Nephew died six weeks after son's birth, 1858
narrative_blocks.body: His nephew Edward Hooker came to East Cambridge to study medicine after graduating Williams College in 1855, but died April 26, 1858 of disease contracted in hospital service — six weeks after Anson's youngest son Edward Dwight was born in the same East Cambridge household on March 17. The same spring brought both a birth and a death into the Hooker physician line.

narrative_blocks.header: Common Council to State House in one career
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond his East Cambridge medical practice, Anson served successively on the Common Council, as Alderman, and as a Representative in the Massachusetts General Court — three ascending levels of civic office held while maintaining his practice from 1822 until his death in 1869. Harvard-trained physicians of his generation were expected to lead their communities as well as treat them.

narrative_blocks.header: First of three consecutive Harvard physician generations, 1822
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Anson Hooker graduated Harvard Medical School in 1822 and built a Cambridge practice that his son Anson Parker inherited after Harvard M.D. 1855, and his grandson Edward Dwight carried on after Harvard M.D. 1884. Three consecutive Hooker physicians trained at Harvard across sixty-two years.

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Parker of East Cambridge MA per EH 1909 #1414.

career.notes: Practiced medicine at Westhampton MA per EH 1909 #529. Established the Hooker physician tradition in Westhampton continued by son Dr. Anson Hooker (H01414, 1799-1869) and through several Westhampton descendants.
Per EH 1909 #529: 'He was a physician and also a trader at West Hampton, Mass.' — supplementing physician income with a trading operation, common pattern for rural New England doctors of the period.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety-four as Westhampton physician
narrative_blocks.body: Born Northampton MA 1766; died Westhampton MA February 27, 1861 at age ninety-four — extraordinary longevity spanning the early Republic through the Civil War's outbreak. Practiced medicine in Westhampton MA and also operated as a trader; both occupations supported a large family of seven children with first wife Anna Thompson before her 1810 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost wife at forty-two, remarried after two years
narrative_blocks.body: First wife Anna Thompson died Westhampton April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving him with seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. William remarried 1812 to Rhoda Phelps, widow of Ensign Hartland of Hartland Connecticut. No documented children from the second marriage; Rhoda predeceased William by fourteen years.

parents.father_name: Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT (per EH 1909 #529)
parents.father_research_notes: Per Edward Hooker 1909 #529: 'm. 1798 [sic — likely 1788], Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor, Conn.' EH date '1798' may be transcription error; her 1786 first child suggests marriage 1785-86. Hugh Thompson East Windsor specific parentage not yet built.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age forty-two leaving seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Died Westhampton MA April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. Her son middle-named for her father Hugh Thompson (Hugh Thompson Hooker, born 1789) carries the East Windsor Connecticut Thompson family name forward.

burialCemetery.name: Cambridge Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Cambridge Middlesex County MA. Burial site of Dr. Anson Hooker (H01414) and descendants. FindAGrave Cemetery #90841.

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
